coredata.dc:title: Light on multi-mode optical properties of carbon dots through rational surface engineering tuning strategies
coredata.dc:creator: Pei, Runfang
He, Pinyi
Qin, Fu
Ma, Jianlong
Xu, Cuixing
Qin, Libo
Gao, Wei
Han, Chun
Wang, Xinyu
Yu, Xu
Bai, Jianliang
Wang, Zhijun
Ren, Lili
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) materials are currently gaining significant attention for applications in bioimaging, fluorescent sensing, anti-counterfeiting, therapeutics, catalysis and other areas. However, efficient and attractive CDs with multi-mode luminescence and tunable spectra through simple modification strategy are still urgently needed for multifunctional optical applications and mechanism exploration. Herein, we demonstrate two rationally designed and completely different CDs surface tuning strategies, which can generate multi-mode optical properties including multi-color fluorescence (FL), room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) and aggregation-induced emission (AIE). The optical properties after weak interactions modification are characterized by freedom and switchability, while the optical properties after strong interaction modification are characterized by compulsion and non-switchability. Moreover, it is further demonstrated that the cell imaging, light-emitting devices (LEDs), “and” logic gate and anti-counterfeiting applications based on multi-mode optical effects. This study not only provides a new model system for understanding the surface states of CDs, but also opens up opportunities for new applications of multi-mode optical materials for various purposes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Surface states
Multi-color fluorescence emission
Room-temperature phosphorescence
Aggregation-induced emission
Multifunctional optical applications

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Novel Materials for Energy and Advanced Applications

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 22275035 ), Scientific and Technological Innovation Program of Colleges and Universities in Shanxi Province ( 2022L507 ) and the Shanxi “1331 Project” Key Innovative Research Team. The authors would like to thank the shiyanjia lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the Raman tests.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The multi-mode optical properties for o, m, p-phenylenediamine derived CDs through surface-states strong/weak interactions tuning strategies and the potential applications of these CDs-based materials.

(a-c) Synthesis routes of m-CDs, o-CDs and p-CDs, and the photographs of m-CDs, o-CDs and p-CDs in toluene (Tol), dichloromethane (CH2Cl2), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), ethyl alcohol (EtOH) and H2O under 365 nm ultraviolet irradiation, respectively. (d-e) Fluorescent p-CDs in aqueous solution (or CH2Cl2 solution) were extracted into CH2Cl2 solution (or aqueous solution) and observed under 365 nm ultraviolet irradiation to study the Red-Green (or Green-Red) fluorescence switching behavior. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a-e) The fluorescence spectra of the same m-CDs excited by different wavelength lights in solvents of Tol (a), CH2Cl2 (b), DMF (c), EtOH (d) and H2O (e). (f-j) The fluorescence spectra of the same o-CDs excited by different wavelength lights in solvents of Tol (f), CH2Cl2 (g), DMF (h), EtOH (i) and H2O (j). (k-o) The fluorescence spectra of the same o-CDs excited by different wavelength lights in solvents of Tol (k), CH2Cl2 (l), DMF (m), EtOH (n) and H2O (o).

(a) Schematic illustration the structures of m-CDs and Urea and the formation route of m-CDs@Urea powders for room-temperature phosphorescence. (b) Photographs of m-CDs@Urea composites captured with a UV lamp (365 nm) on or off. (c) The fluorescence and phosphorescence emission spectra of m-CDs@Urea powders. (d) The room-temperature phosphorescence decay spectrum of the m-CDs@Urea powders.

(a) Schematic illustration the synthesis of p-CDs-SA. (b) UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra for p-CDs EtOH solutions. Inset: the fluorescence photograph of p-CDs in EtOH under UV lamp. (c) UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra for p-CDs-SA powders. Inset: The fluorescence photographs of p-CDs-SA powders (left) and p-CDs-SA DMSO solutions (right) under UV lamp.

Schematic illustration of the different luminescence mechanism: Compared with the multi-color emission and room-temperature phosphorescence optical properties obtained by the surface-state weak interaction tuning of CDs, the aggregation-induced emission optical properties can be obtained by the strong interaction tuning.

(a) Security protection application. (b) Fluorescence images of p-CDs in HeLa cells under different conditions. The first line is fluorescence images of HeLa cells without p-CDs (control group). The second line is fluorescence images of HeLa cells adding p-CDs (60 μg/mL). (c) CIE coordinates of a yellow LED. (d) Fluorescence emission spectrum of a yellow LED. Inset: Optical picture of the yellow LED device. (e) The truth table for AND logic gates. (f-g) The fluorescence images of p-CDs in EtOH (marked symbol “I”), p-CDs-SA in DMSO (marked symbol “♡”) and m-CDs in EtOH (marked symbol “U”) under room temperature and low temperature (-18 °C), respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Light on multi-mode optical properties of carbon dots through rational surface engineering tuning strategies

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Life Sciences, Changzhi University, Changzhi 046011, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Life Sciences, Changzhi University, Changzhi 046011, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Southeast University, Nanjing 211189, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Southeast University, Nanjing 211189, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Changzhi University, Changzhi 046011, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Changzhi University, Changzhi 046011, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) materials are currently gaining significant attention for applications in bioimaging, fluorescent sensing, anti-counterfeiting, therapeutics, catalysis and other areas. However, efficient and attractive CDs with multi-mode luminescence and tunable spectra through simple modification strategy are still urgently needed for multifunctional optical applications and mechanism exploration. Herein, we demonstrate two rationally designed and completely different CDs surface tuning strategies, which can generate multi-mode optical properties including multi-color fluorescence (FL), room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) and aggregation-induced emission (AIE). The optical properties after weak interactions modification are characterized by freedom and switchability, while the optical properties after strong interaction modification are characterized by compulsion and non-switchability. Moreover, it is further demonstrated that the cell imaging, light-emitting devices (LEDs), “and” logic gate and anti-counterfeiting applications based on multi-mode optical effects. This study not only provides a new model system for understanding the surface states of CDs, but also opens up opportunities for new applications of multi-mode optical materials for various purposes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), which are a new type of quasi-spherical fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterial, have received considerable attention owing to extraordinary optical properties, non-toxicity, ease of syntheses and flexible structure [1–3]. These carbon-based surface passivated nanostructures compete with other related optical materials, including conventional fluorescent dyes, semiconductor quantum dots and perovskite quantum dots, with a long list of benefits and emerging applications. Over the past twenty years or so, CDs and CDs-based composite materials have developed vigorously and rapidly. Using the optical properties of fluorescence (FL), thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) and room temperature phosphorescence (RTP), numerous breakthroughs have been made in different fields, such as chemistry, biology, medicine, environment and physics [3–6]. With the in-depth study of emerging and advanced applications, CDs can be applied to biomedicine including sensing [7,8], imaging [9–11], drug-delivery [12] and photodynamic therapy [13], anti-counterfeiting [4–6], catalysis [14–18], light-emitting diodes (LEDs) [19,20] and energy harvesting in solar cells [21].

Exactly similar to natural species require highly diverse and complex photonic structures to adapt to the stimuli and changes in environmental systems, the easy modification of luminous chromophores responsible for luminescence has special significance for the development of luminescent materials with more diverse and attractive luminescence features and functions [22–25]. Abundant precursors endow CDs with versatile structures and properties [26]. However, the complex luminescence mechanism of CDs is still unclear and controversial. Specifically, there is no precise structural design for the synthesis process, and controlling fluorescence and producing long-lived luminescence is difficult; in addition, the connections between these properties are still vague [3]. Therefore, it remains a huge challenge to control an arbitrary chromophore of CDs to emit light. That is to say, it is quite difficult to achieve multi-mode luminescence in CDs-based materials. In recent years, a series of complex optical properties such as multi-color fluorescence [27–32], RTP [33–40], TADF [40,41] and aggregate induced emission (AIE) [42–47] of CDs and their applications have been reported. However, to our knowledge, switching from one luminescence mode to another through simple modification or tuning strategies, which can realize the interesting multi-mode luminous optical properties of CDs, has been rarely reported.

Based on the above considerations, in this work, we report two distinct surface tuning strategies for obtaining multi-mode luminescence properties of CDs, named “Lock/Unlock tuning strategy” (weak interaction tuning strategy) and “Lock tuning strategy” (strong interaction tuning strategy). Unlike previous single luminescence properties obtained through the surface tuning strategy of CDs, the luminescence properties of CDs can achieve multi-color fluorescence, RTP and AIE related solid-state fluorescence (SSF) optical properties of under reasonably designed and completely different surface tuning strategies, exhibiting multi-mode characteristics of optical properties (Fig. 1 ). At the same time, we compare the effect of two tuning strategies on the intrinsic state luminescence of CDs in detail for the first time. Furthermore, we exploit them potential applications in anti-counterfeiting, cell imaging, LEDs and logic gate. Reasonable surface-state tuning strategy provides an effective way to construct multi-mode optical platform and understand the luminescence mechanism of CDs.

In a wide variety of choices, phenylenediamine (PD) has been active in the forefront of precursors for the preparation of CDs [28–31,48–52]. As shown in Fig. 2 , the preparation of FL m-CDs, o-CDs and p-CDs can be readily accomplished through solvothermal/hydrothermal reaction, specifically, heating MPD, OPD and PPD in EtOH/H2O solutions in autoclaves, respectively, and then purified by using silica gel column chromatography method (see experimental section for details). The morphologies of three luminescent CDs were investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Fig. S1
a-f clearly show that all three materials exhibit a uniform distribution and nanoparticles with average sizes of approximately 7.48 nm (m-CDs), 6.37 nm (o-CDs) and 1.97 nm (p-CDs). The Raman spectra in Fig. S2 displays two peaks at 1386 cm−1 (D band) and 1519 cm−1 (G band), referring to areas of sp2 and sp3 hybrid orbitals of C atom, respectively. The 2D peaks of the three CDs can also be found at around 2799 cm−1, indicating the existence of a few graphene layer [53–55]. The intensity ratios (IG/ID) of m-CDs, o-CDs and p-CDs are 1.03, 1.14 and 1.11, respectively. The distinct G band peaks suggest that m-CDs, o-CDs and p-CDs have a certain graphitization degree [56].

Then the chemical bonds and functional groups of the m-CDs, o-CDs and p-CDs were further identified by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra. As shown in Fig. S3 , the FT-IR measurements spectra of the three CDs are similar which reveals the existence of a similar chemical composition of the CDs. The typical peak at 3380 cm−1 corresponds to –OH group stretching. The peak around 3199 cm−1 is related to the –NH group. The characteristic peaks at 2920 cm−1 corresponding to –CH stretching vibration, and those around 1559 and 1840 cm−1 correspond to CO, CN and CC bonds. The characteristic peaks at 1522 cm−1 and 1124 cm−1 corresponding to CN stretching vibration and CO stretching vibration. The above results reveal that all three CDs are composed of aromatic structure, and surface are rich in –NH2 and –OH. Then the functional groups of three CDs are further identified by XPS. As shown in Fig. S4 , XPS full spectra of three CDs demonstrates three characteristic peaks near 285, 400 and 531 eV, corresponding to C 1s, N 1s and O 1s, respectively. The percentages of C 1s, N 1s and O 1s of m-CDs, o-CDs and p-CDs are listed in Table S1 . As shown in Fig. S5
a, d and g, the high-resolution C 1s spectra of three CDs can be fitted into three binding energy peaks of 284.1, 285.5 and 287.3 eV, corresponding to the presence of CC/CC, CN/CO and CN/CO binds, respectively [57]. In Fig. S5
b, e and h, the spectra of N 1s can be decomposed into three peaks at 399.3, 400.3, and 401.6 eV, which are attributed to pyridine N, amino N, and graphitic N, respectively [57]. The O1s spectra in Fig. S5
c, f and i exhibit two peaks at 531.8 and 533.1 eV corresponding to CO and CO bands, respectively [57]. FT-IR and XPS characterization confirmed the existence of a large number of easily modified organic functional groups such as –NH2 and –OH on the surface of three CDs, which play an important role in the regulation of multi-mode optical properties.

The optical performance of x-CDs (x = m, o or p) was verified by UV–Vis absorption ( Fig. S6 ) and FL spectra (Fig. 3 ). Depicted in Fig. 2 a-c, under UV irradiation, fifteen CDs solutions exhibit bright colors from blue to red for samples of m-CDs, o-CDs and p-CDs, respectively. As shown in Fig. S6 , the UV–Vis absorption spectra of three CDs show obvious absorption in the ultraviolet and visible regions. The m-CDs, o-CDs and p-CDs exhibit obvious peaks near 254 and 293 nm were attributed to the π-π* transitions of CC and CN, while the absorption located at 300–350 nm can be assigned to the n-π* transition of CO and CN [48]. Interestingly, m-CD, o-CDs and p-CDs exhibit extra absorption peaks near 460, 440 or 504 nm, which may come from some conjugate groups on the surface of CDs [48].

Fig. 3 shows that the FL emission wavelengths of m-CDs, o-CDs and p-CDs under different excitation wavelengths and in different solvents, respectively. FL peaks position of m-CDs varied with different excitation wavelengths, while o-CDs and p-CDs showed excitation-independent emissions. As shown in Fig. 3 a, b, d and e, the FL emission peaks red-shifted significantly at different excitation wavelengths. For example, the FL emission peaks of m-CDs in aqueous solution at different excitation wavelengths (320, 340, 360, 380, 400, 420, 440 and 460 nm) were tested. Obviously, under different excitation wavelengths, the FL emission wavelengths shifted from 502 to 523 nm. Interestingly, the as-synthesized m-CDs show two emissions at 435 and 497 nm, and excitation-independent FL behavior in DMF solution (Fig. 3 c). As shown in Fig. 3 f-o, the FL emission of o-CDs and p-CDs exhibited excitation-independence in solvents with polarities, respectively. For instance, the emission maximum of the o-CDs in an aqueous solution remained unchanged at 558 nm when varying the excitation wavelength from 340 to 480 nm; similarly, the emission maximum of the p-CDs in an aqueous solution remained unchanged at 610 nm when varying the excitation wavelength from 360 to 500 nm. The next research aims to reveal the different FL emission characteristics of CDs affected by weak interactions such as hydrogen-bond and dipole–dipole interactions [29]. As shown in Figs. 2 and 3, in normal circumstances, the FL emission peaks red-shifted as the solvent polarity increased. The m-CDs, o-CDs and p-CDs derived from three isomers of PD exhibit a broader color tunable range of 410–610 nm, covering the full color from indigo blue to red. The FT-IR and XPS characterization show that the three CDs have abundant oxygen and nitrogen-containing sub structures like –OH and –NH2 on the surface of CDs. According to previous investigations, the surface states affect the FL mechanism [31,58], the solvent-dependent properties of CDs in protic solvents are mainly due to the hydrogen bond interaction between protic solvent and CDs, and dipole–dipole interaction plays a major role in different aprotic solvents [59]. In solvents of toluene and CH2Cl2, the dipole–dipole interactions can constitute steady excited states leading to short wavelength emission of CDs. In DMF, the weak hydrogen bond interactions between solvents and CDs can generate new stable energy level, which can form relatively long wavelength emission. In solvents of EtOH and H2O, the –OH and –NH2 groups of CDs can form strong hydrogen bond interactions with solvents, leading to gradual decrease in the emission energy level and spectra red shifts [29]. We next consider why the weak interaction on the surface of CDs tends to exhibits fluorescence switching behavior based on intrinsic photoluminescence. As shown in Fig. S7 , fluorescent switchability character can be operated by extraction experiment (H2O and CH2Cl2), the FL spectra of p-CDs (extracted from aqueous solution of p-CDs) in CH2Cl2 are basically similar to those of directly dispersed CH2Cl2 (Fig. 3 l and
Fig. S7
a). On the contrary, the FL spectra of p-CDs (extracted from CH2Cl2 solution of p-CDs) in aqueous solution are basically similar to those of directly dispersed H2O (Fig. 3 o and
Fig. S7
b). These results indicate that weak interactions such as hydrogen bonding in protic solvents or dipole–dipole interaction in aprotic solvents is considered the “key” to unlocking the CDs’ surface state atmosphere and thus switching the fluorescence behaviors of CDs [60,61].

Due to the high density hydrogen bonds, poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA), cyanuric acid (CA), melamine (MA) and urea are often used as the matrix to afford organic and CDs-based RTP materials with long lifetime and high efficiency [6,33–38,62–66]. In view of excellent water-solubility and the abundant amino of urea, the hydrogen bond interaction between urea and the phosphors is in favor of boosting RTP properties. As shown in Fig. 4
a, –OH and –NH2 groups of m-CDs are able to form hydrogen bonds with a selected solid matrix of urea. The fixation of m-CDs in urea matrix would facilitate their triplet emission (RTP). The obtained m-CDs@Urea powders can emit blue-white luminescence upon UV (365 nm) illumination and then emit turquoise phosphorescence after 365 nm UV illumination ceased (Fig. 4 b and Movie 1 ). In order to further understand the phosphorescence properties of m-CDs@Urea, the RTP emission and lifetime decay profiles spectra were also measured (Fig. 4 c and d). The long-lived phosphorescence emission center was located at ≈481 nm under 365 nm excitation wavelength (Fig. 4 c). Fig. 4 d reveals the afterglow decay spectrum of m-CDs@Urea powders, which has to be fitted with a tri-exponential function with lifetime components to be 28, 218, and 1101 ms, respectively ( Table S2 ). According to the following equation [34],
(1)
τ
avg
=
∑
α i τ
i 2
/
∑
α i τ i

The average lifetime of the sample was calculated to be 1006 ms (Fig. 4 d). The concentration-dependent optical properties of m-CDs@Urea are studied to observe the FL emission peaks in different concentrations ( Fig. S8 ). The m-CDs@Urea powders showed FL maxima at 445 nm (Fig. 4 c), and 1.000, 0.125, 0.016 and 0.002 g/mL m-CDs@Urea for examples, the maximum emission wavelengths are 484, 491, 493 and 504 nm, respectively ( Fig. S8 ). The FL spectra reveals that with the dilution of sample concentration, the hydrogen bond force between m-CDs and urea is weakened, while the hydrogen bond force between m-CDs and water molecules is strengthened, and the m-CDs can still maintain their intrinsic state luminescence properties.

By adding salicylaldehyde (SA) into ethanol solution of p-CDs, aldehyde group and amino group on the surface of CDs can form imine bonds through dehydration, so as to investigate the effect of strong interaction modification on the surface state of CDs. As shown in Fig. 5
a, after the addition of SA in p-CDs solution, the fluorescence obviously changed from red of p-CDs solution to yellow of p-CDs-SA powders (Fig. 5 b and c). The aldehyde groups of SA can form a CN binding Schiff base reaction with the amino groups of p-CDs through dehydration [44,47]. The FT-IR spectrum of p-CDs-SA is shown in Fig. S9 , after functionalization with the SA-based ligands, the CN stretching bands at 1674 cm−1 increases the intensity, whilst new peaks appeared at 732 cm−1 associated with Ar-H vibrations, confirming that the ligands were successfully attached to the p-CDs [44]. As presented in Fig. 5 c, p-CDs-SA is non-luminous when dissolved in a good solvent such as DMSO, while Y-CDs is significantly luminous in the solid state. The possible AIE luminescence mechanism of p-CDs-SA is that when p-CDs-SA in the aggregation state, the surface ligands formed by SA prevent the aggregation of carbon core of p-CDs, thus avoiding the aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) phenomenon caused by π-π stacking interactions. Hence, these results show that strong interactions, such as covalent bonds, are considered to “lock” the surface state of the p-CDs, so that the FL behavior changes from ACQ to AIE, resulting in difficult restoration of the CDs’ intrinsic state luminescence properties.

The surface state regulation composed of strong and weak interactions provides an ideal platform for the multi-mode luminescence. Two luminescence mechanisms were proposed under different intensity chemical tool tuning conditions (Fig. 6 ): 1) Hydrogen bond and dipole–dipole interactions were introduced to obtain the optical properties such as multi-color fluorescence and RTP; 2) Covalent bond was introduced to obtain the optical property of AIE. Notably, CDs modified by the weak interactions still maintain the intrinsic chemical structure, and the optical properties can still be restored by “Lock/Unlock” strategy. However, CDs modified by the strong interaction changes the intrinsic chemical structure, and it is difficult to restore the optical properties of CDs themselves.

In consideration of the establishment of CDs-based multi-mode optical platform and the full-color emissions, RTP and AIE luminescence behaviors, the potential applications of anti-counterfeiting, cell imaging, LEDs and “and” logic gate are investigated. The m-CDs@Urea powders with RTP optical properties could be developed for promising applications in data encryption (Fig. 7
a) [38]. The patterned security feature of “Y” was filled phosphorescent m-CDs@Urea powders, while “B” and “D” were filled with fluorescent o-CDs@Urea powders. The “Y” was encrypted in “BYD” under 365 nm UV light excitation because of the blue-white fluorescence of m-CDs@Urea and yellow fluorescence of o-CDs@Urea. However, when the UV light is turned off, the encryption of “Y” could be readily visualized.

Since p-CDs have red fluorescence with low-toxicity, we explored their potential applications as fluorescent bioimaging agents. First, we chose the CCK8 assay to evaluate the cytotoxicity of p-CDs on HeLa cells. According to the analysis of the test results, the cell viability was close to 90 % after incubation at 100 μg/mL for 24 h without light ( Fig. S10 ), indicating that p-CDs had low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility for HeLa cells. Subsequently, 60 μg/mL p-CDs are stained with HeLa cells to explore their FL bioimaging capabilities. Under the excitation of a confocal laser, the morphology of HeLa cells was not damaged and emitted bright red fluorescence (Fig. 7 b), indicating that p-CDs could smoothly cross the cell membrane and label HeLa cells, which also fully confirmed the potential of p-CDs as a fluorescent probe for live cell imaging.

The p-CDs-SA powders are used to produce yellow color luminous LED. In the production process of solid LED, glue A was mixed with the p-CDs-SA powders and then mixed with glue B. The mixed material was coated on UV 460 nm chip, and then dried for 3 h, and yellow LED devices are fabricated. Fig. 7 d shows an optical image of the LED device that showed bright yellow under a constant current. As shown in Fig. 7 d, the electroluminescence (EL) spectrum corresponding to the yellow LED is given, and it can be seen that there is an obvious yellow main peak a ∼584 nm. The CIE coordinates of this yellow light LED device is (0.48, 0.41) (Fig. 7 c). The results show that the yellow color p-CDs-SA powders have a certain potential in the field of optoelectronic devices.

With the decrease of temperature, the DMSO solution system of p-CDs-SA gradually crystallized and showed bright yellow fluorescence emission. This exciting phenomenon is due to the apparent hardening of the p-CDs-SA structures by solvent crystallization, which limits the movement of the molecules, reduces the non-radiative transition process, and enables p-CDs-SA to emit fluoresce effectively [67]. If both the UV lamp and the temperature changes are considered as on/off operations, we can design a simple “And” logic gate as shown in Fig. 7 e-g [68]. For an “I♡U”/low temperature system, the UV irradiation and −18 °C are considered as inputs, and the changes of the red, yellow or cyan fluorescence are used as the output modes I, II or Ⅲ, respectively. For the inputs, the presence and absence of UV irradiation or −18 °C are defined as “1” and “0”, respectively. For output I, II and Ⅲ, we can define the non-fluorescence color of p-CDs in EtOH, p-CDs-SA in DMSO and m-CDs in EtOH as “0”, while the red, yellow and cyan fluorescence color as “1”. According to discussed above, only the presence of both inputs (1/1) could cause a visual color “I” with red fluorescence, “♡” with yellow fluorescence and “U” with cyan fluorescence (output “1” / “1” / “1”). This phenomenon is exactly as a Chinese proverb says: “without a cold biting cold, how fragrant plum blossom?” Love may be similar.

In summary, we have developed two strategies for the rational modification of the surface of CDs, which fully combines the features of surface-states and functional groups and thus enables the modulation of multi-color fluorescence, RTP and AIE optical properties. Two kinds of surface tuning tools, weak interactions (hydrogen bond and dipole–dipole interactions) and strong interaction (covalent bond) are used to manipulate the surface state change of CDs, resulting in build a bridge between surface states and multi-mode luminescence properties. Through the weak interaction tuning strategy, the reversible adjustment effect of CDs surface-state can be realized, which is conducive to exploring the switchability of optical properties. On the contrary, the irreversible adjustment of the surface-states of CDs can be realized by using the strong interaction tuning strategy, which is conducive to exploring the non-switchability of optical properties. As a result of the unexpected multi-color fluorescence, RTP and AIE emission obtained through surface engineering tuning strategies, CDs as chromophores can establish a multi-mode luminescence platform, which is quite promising for anti-counterfeiting, cell imaging, LEDs, “and” logic gate, and other potential applications. In addition, the concept of strong or weak surface modification of CDs also provides a valuable idea for the research and development of novel multi-purpose nano-materials.

Runfang Pei: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Pinyi He: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Fu Qin: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Jianlong Ma: Investigation, Formal analysis. Cuixing Xu: Investigation, Formal analysis. Libo Qin: Methodology, Investigation. Wei Gao: Investigation, Formal analysis. Chun Han: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Xinyu Wang: Investigation, Formal analysis. Xu Yu: Methodology, Formal analysis. Jianliang Bai: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Zhijun Wang: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Lili Ren: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22275035), Scientific and Technological Innovation Program of Colleges and Universities in Shanxi Province (2022L507) and the Shanxi “1331 Project” Key Innovative Research Team. The authors would like to thank the shiyanjia lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the Raman tests.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2024.149459.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary video 2

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon nanodots from an in silico perspective

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The light of carbon dots: From mechanism to applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Wang, S. Lu, The light of carbon dots: From mechanism to applications, Matter 5 (2022) 110-149.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots in zeolites: A new class of thermally activated delayed fluorescence materials with ultralong lifetimes
